prompt:
Identity-platform ticket. Our nightly HR-to-Okta user sync is a shell script with a hardcoded SSWS token that got rate-limited into the ground last quarter-end and then double-created three contractors when someone re-ran it. Rebuild the transport layer properly as a small Python package oktasync/ (stdlib only, python3, no pip installs): OAuth service-app auth instead of the static token, honest rate-limit handling, real pagination, and a sync that stops instead of guessing when Okta pushes back on a create. test_okta_transport.py is the acceptance contract; it and everything under docs/ are protected.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Okta docs — the OAuth-for-Okta service app guide (the token endpoint, which client authentication method service apps must use, the assertion's exact claims and accepted algorithms, the form fields, what comes back), Okta's rate-limit monitoring page (the three header names and, critically, the units of the reset value), the Core API page for how Link-header pagination is meant to be consumed, and enough of the Users API reference to know what POST /api/v1/users does by DEFAULT if you don't say otherwise — our last sync emailed 40 contractors at 2am because nobody checked. First-party Okta sources only (developer.okta.com or Okta's published API spec). docs/contract.json pins the wire contract our mock speaks, docs/official_sources.json the provenance; if live docs contradict a pin, flag it in your summary and leave protected files alone. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests import (internal layout beyond this is yours, 3+ modules expected):
- oktasync.auth.ClientCredentialsTokenSource(org_url, client_id, scopes, signer, clock) — builds the documented token request. The signature is delegated: signer(signing_input_bytes) -> raw signature bytes stands in for our KMS-held key, so construct the assertion segments yourself (header with the RSA alg the docs accept + typ, claims per the guide with a 300s lifetime from clock.now()) and base64url the signer's output as the third segment. Tokens cache until 60s before expiry (clock-driven, expires_in from the response) and can be invalidated.
- oktasync.transport.OktaTransport(org_url, token_source, clock, wait) with get_json(path_or_url), create(path, body), paginate(path) (generator following the documented Link mechanism — heads-up: the next URLs Okta returns aren't always shaped like what you sent), and a rate_limit property exposing the last-seen limit/remaining/reset_epoch from the headers Okta stamps on every response. On 401 with the documented invalid-token errorCode: refresh once, retry once, then raise oktasync.transport.OktaAuthError (message names the errorCode, never any token). On 429 for a GET: wait exactly until the advertised reset — anchored on the response's own Date header, not the machine clock — through the injected wait callable, retry the same URL, at most two retries, then raise RateLimitError (exposes reset_epoch; message has the errorCode). On 429 for create(): raise RateLimitError immediately — a user create is not idempotent and must never be blindly retried.
- oktasync.sync.sync_users(transport, desired_profiles) — list existing users (paginated), match by the documented identity field, create only the missing ones as STAGED users (mind that create default!), and on a rate-limited create return {'created': [...], 'existing': [...], 'halted': {'login': ..., 'reset_epoch': ...}} so the operator resumes after the window instead of re-running blind ('halted': None when everything lands).

Acceptance: python3 test_okta_transport.py — must print the final OK line. Loopback mock only; dummy client id; no sleeps (all waiting goes through the injected callable) and no wall-clock reads.

research.product: Okta Workforce Identity Cloud — Core API transport (OAuth 2.0 for Okta, rate limits, Users API)
research.interface: /api/v1 + /oauth2/v1/token, Okta Core management REST API (official OpenAPI spec 2026.07.1)

official_sources.title: Implement OAuth for Okta with a service app | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: POST https://{org}/oauth2/v1/token with grant_type=client_credentials; private_key_jwt is the only supported client auth method for OAuth-for-Okta service apps; client_assertion_type urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer; JWT claims aud={token URL}, iss=sub=client_id, exp max one hour, alg RS256/384/512 or ES256/384/512; form-encoded body; space-separated okta.* scopes; response token_type=Bearer, expires_in=3600, access_token; usage via Authorization: Bearer

official_sources.title: Monitoring and troubleshooting rate limits | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: X-Rate-Limit-Limit (ceiling for the current request), X-Rate-Limit-Remaining (requests left in the window), X-Rate-Limit-Reset (reset time in UTC epoch SECONDS); 429 when a bucket is exceeded; throttle/retry guidance anchored on the reset value

official_sources.title: Core Okta API | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: org base URL pattern, Bearer/SSWS auth schemes, application/json media types, Link rel="next"/rel="self" cursor pagination with the instruction to follow returned link URLs because cursor and query formats can change without notice, error envelope errorCode/errorSummary/errorLink/errorId/errorCauses

official_sources.title: okta/okta-management-openapi-spec — Okta Admin Management API (version 2026.07.1)
official_sources.used_for: listUsers (GET /api/v1/users, limit default 200, opaque after cursor from the Link header, oauth2 scope okta.users.read); createUser (POST /api/v1/users, activate query param DEFAULT TRUE, scope okta.users.manage); error codes E0000047 (429) and E0000011 (invalid token, 401)

official_sources.title: Okta API Error Codes | Okta Developer
official_sources.used_for: E0000011 = 401 'Invalid token provided'; E0000047 = 429 rate limit exceeded

research.language_rationale: The HR-driven user/group sync this transport feeds is a Python service; Okta documents the token exchange and the management API as plain HTTP (form-encoded OAuth request, JSON REST, Link headers), so urllib from the stdlib exercises the documented wire contract directly — the RS256 signature itself is delegated to an injected signer exactly as production delegates it to a KMS, keeping the hermetic tests free of crypto dependencies.
